svg.text: Cart-Pole Control on a Network of Analog Spiking Neurons
Felix [Surname]¹, Jochem [Surname]², Vincent [Surname]³ · Experimental Research Project (NS-205B), BSc Physics, Utrecht University

text.tspan: A cart-pole is balanced by a small network of physical
analog leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) neurons. Instead of
the neuron's linear regime, each neuron is read as a single
bit — firing or silent — so its threshold becomes a sign()
activation. Control stays robust to the noisy, low-resolution
spike rates of analog hardware, and just three neurons
are enough to stabilise the pole.

svg.text: Key idea: one bit per neuron
makes control immune to spike-rate noise.

text.tspan: Neuromorphic computing emulates neural dynamics
directly in physical circuits that communicate through
sparse spikes, enabling event-driven, low-power
computation. Its canonical unit is the leaky integrate-
and-fire neuron. The Lu.i board is a low-cost, fully analog
LIF neuron that can be wired into networks — but its
spike rates are noisy and low-resolution, which makes
functional computation on the hardware a challenge.
Can a handful of physical analog neurons control an
unstable system in real time? Prior work used the LIF
linear regime for LQR control, but rate imprecision forced
large neuron populations. We ask whether the threshold
nonlinearity itself can serve as the computation.

g.text: Deploy a trained network on physical Lu.i LIF neurons.
Use the firing threshold as a binary sign() activation.
Rate-encode the state and decode 1-bit firing decisions.
1. Stradmann et al. (2025) Trends Neurosci. Educ. 38, 100248.
2. Banerjee et al. (2025) Neuromorph. Comput. Eng. 5, 044006.

text.tspan: A fully connected 3–3–1 network of Lu.i neurons. The
cart-pole state variables are rate-encoded about a 10 Hz
baseline (v ↦ 10 + 5v Hz) and delivered as pulse trains.
Each neuron sums its synaptic inputs (potentiometer
weights) and fires when its membrane crosses threshold;
this firing is read as a ±1 sign activation. Hidden
decisions are re-encoded to drive the output neuron,
whose sign sets a two-level (bang-bang) force. Weights are
trained offline in a simulator and transferred to the boards;
a baseline calibration and a parallel software reference
validate the analog hardware in real time.

svg.text: Illustrative panels — replace each with your measured time series (e.g. pole angle, cart position, control force vs. time).
Add your key finding here — e.g. hardware vs.
noise, chatter) and outline future work.
Experimental Research Project (NS-205B) · BSc Physics · Utrecht University · 2026
